raw_consumer_statement:
I went to XXXX XXXX on XX/XX/year>. 
In the airport is a small XXXX store. 

I bought a few things like XXXX XXXX XXXX etc. 

The cashier did not speak great English. 
She showed me the price on the calculator at XXXX pesos, around $ XXXX USD. It was a bit much but I bought anyways. She had a card scanner on like a phone tablet. She scanned my card then asked for a signature. 
The signature page was like a blank line on the tablet and didnt show itemized prices or the final price. 

They did not have receipts to print off. 

I let and got a notice on my phone that they charged me {$7500.00} pesos not {$750.00} pesos. 

I went back to the store minutes later. The cashiers manager said no refunds but I can pick out items to make up the difference for the extra $ XXXX USD I was charged. I said no and again asked for a receipt. He said they can not print them. 
I immediately called American Express to notify them. 

They said they would investigate. Weeks later I get a letter in the mail saying I lost the appeal and the vendor submitted a receipt. 
It was a receipt with the price of XXXX pesos ( not itemized ) and my signature below. My signature was clearly photoshopped from the tablet I signed, Im guessing they have signatures saved. I never signed that receipt with that price. 

Its blatant fraud on the vendors part. I requested Amex ask for video footage which would show the incident. They said they would not do that as the video would suffice. 

I would like assistance challenging this. If someone can just photoshop your signature onto a receipt then we have no defense against this. 


Please reach out for additional information. 

Thank you
